doc: Per-method classification used by the website to render Detailed Results chips. Each method carries (a) a heaviness level anchored on the Python baseline wall time at the published bench scale (~2 MiB text input, 3 iters, 1 worker per cf6e8e7), and (b) an optional lifecycle status (active/deprecated/retired) with the version it changed in. Match is case-insensitive substring on the bench label; longest match wins. Notes additionally describe peak-RAM-at-scale where it differs from bench-scale heaviness — e.g. kFMe/kFAe are medium at 2 MiB but can become extreme on multi-GiB files.

low.label: Low
low.explanation: Under 100 ms in Python at bench scale. Cheap and bounded. Safe to run on any host.

medium.label: Medium
medium.explanation: 100 ms to 1 s in Python at bench scale. Modest CPU + RAM. Fine on laptops.

high.label: High
high.explanation: 1 s to 5 s in Python at bench scale, or hundreds of MiB peak RSS. Use a dedicated build box if your laptop is small.

extreme.label: Extreme
extreme.explanation: Over 5 s in Python at bench scale, or multi-GiB peak RSS. Do not run on machines under 32 GiB RAM for production-scale inputs.

deprecated.label: Deprecated
deprecated.explanation: Public API stays callable so existing blobs / call sites keep working, but the method is no longer recommended for new code. Emits a deprecation warning at compile time and (where applicable) at first call. See CHANGELOG for the alternative.

retired.label: Retired
retired.explanation: Same as deprecated, plus a one-time runtime banner with the retirement story. Existing blobs still decode; nothing new is built on top. Will be removed in a future major bump.

methods.notes: SHA-512, fixed-size output. ~7 ms in Python at 2 MiB — near the OpenSSL throughput ceiling; C++/Java are within 30%.

Deprecated in 3.7.0 — non-standard chained hash (SHA-256 → SHA-1 → SHA-512 → SHA-256). Includes a SHA-1 hop with known collision weaknesses; output is 256 bits despite the '513' name. ~8 ms in Python at 2 MiB. Use hash512 (SHA-512) or SHA3-512 for new code.
stdlib base64 — memory-bandwidth bound. ~21 ms in Python at 2 MiB.
Lightweight AEAD variant — Argon2id at reduced cost + AES-256-GCM. ~180 ms in Python at 2 MiB; the KDF dominates wall time.
Deprecated in 3.7.0 — SHA-256 with a custom prefilter (no added security). ~280 ms in Python at 2 MiB (prefilter walk is pure-Python). Use hash512 / uhash513 instead.

Encodes a file as a carrier PNG (audio source) or WAV (image source) — lossless container packing for steganographic transport. ~440 ms in Python at 2 MiB; C++ is ~17× faster because Python's Pillow PNG encoder has per-pixel dispatch overhead libpng doesn't. Output is structurally whole-buffer (one carrier file per input), so streaming isn't a fix path here. RAM scales with file size; multi-GiB inputs land in the EXTREME tier.
Inverse of kFMe — decodes the carrier PNG/WAV back to original bytes. Same bench profile and same scale-up RAM caveat.

Streaming AEAD with chunked frames (1 MiB chunks) + optional ffmpeg pipe. ~470 ms in Python at 2 MiB. Note: Java is currently SLOWER than Python here because the small bench input doesn't amortize the per-frame AES-GCM Cipher init overhead; at 200 MiB Java overtakes by ~3×.
Legacy PNG-only carrier encoder (kFMe with the auto-detection removed). ~480 ms in Python at 2 MiB; same structural Pillow vs. libpng gap as kFMe. Marked legacy: prefer kFMe for new code.
Inverse of kFAe — decodes the legacy PNG carrier. Same bench profile.
Inverse of AN7. ~500 ms in Python at 2 MiB. Java is unexpectedly slower at this scale; see an7 note.

AN7 stealth anonymisation — chunked AES-CTR + sparse-flip + super-block permutation. ~520 ms in Python at 2 MiB. At this size Java is ~1.8× slower than Python because the per-chunk Cipher.init overhead doesn't amortize over only 2 chunks; the Java path is already optimized (cached ThreadLocal Cipher + Mac, in-place AES-CTR per commit 92a8a68). At ≥50 MiB inputs Java overtakes Python decisively.
Full AEAD — Argon2id at default cost + AES-256-GCM. ~710 ms in Python at 2 MiB; the KDF is ~90% of wall time and cannot be sped up without weakening security parameters.

Numeric encoding — turns arbitrary bytes into fixed-10-digit blocks with embedded FNV-1a checksum, for channels that only carry digits (SMS, phone fields, numeric-only DB columns). ~1.5 s in Python at 2 MiB. The bottleneck is the Python interpreter dispatch loop: ~524k iterations × ~4 arithmetic ops per iteration approaches the CPython speed ceiling for this algorithm. The arithmetic itself is on machine-sized integers (not bigints over the whole buffer), so NumPy can't help — uint64 overflows during the multiply step. The real fix is a C extension; PyPy already runs this ~30× faster than CPython by JIT-compiling the same loop. C++/Java are 18–30× faster than CPython for the same reason. Active because digit-only output has a legitimate purpose; the chip honestly reflects the interpreter gap.

🫡 Retired in 3.7.0. First encoding method in BaseFWX (V1, proof-of-concept era). Non-streaming whole-buffer NumPy path; peak RSS ≈ 5–6× input. ~1.7 s in Python at 2 MiB (C++ is 8.8× faster). Use base64 / hash512 for new code. Existing blobs still decode. ❤️

Deprecated in 3.7.0 — reversible obfuscation codec with no security goal; ~2.8 s in Python at 2 MiB because it calls the (retired) b256 internally. C++ is 13.8× faster. Use stdlib base64 for new code.

Password-keyed AEAD over a whole-buffer string — URL-safe output. ~3.2 s in Python at 2 MiB; the PBKDF2 step dominates. Sibling of b512 (password required, URL-safe base64); see CHANGELOG for the pick-one-of comparison.
Password-or-master-keyed AEAD over a whole-buffer string — standard base64 output. ~3.2 s in Python at 2 MiB; PBKDF2-bound. Sibling of pb512.
Image cipher — whole-pixel-buffer AEAD. Heaviness scales with image dimensions.
File-mode wrapper around b512 — KDF + AEAD over the whole file. ~7.3 s in Python at the bench input; multi-GiB files extend linearly.
File-mode wrapper around pb512. ~8.6 s in Python at the bench input; multi-GiB files extend linearly.
